data.title: Microsoft's stock has been in a rut since earnings. What's going on?
data.description:
By Emily Bary

Analysts offer a few theories for the seemingly 'unexplainable' way the tech giant's stock has fallen out of favor in recent weeks

Microsoft has fallen out of favor with Big Tech investors in recent weeks.

Microsoft Corp. had arguably the strongest earnings report within Big Tech last quarter, at least relative to investor expectations. Yet its stock is flat since just before the results came out, as nearly every other member of the "Magnificent Seven" has moved higher.

Executives at Microsoft (MSFT) "sound fantastic in buy-side meetings as of late," according to Mizuho desk-based analyst Jordan Klein, and the company is levered to OpenAI's booming demand as well as upbeat cloud trends even outside of artificial intelligence. Microsoft has also told investors what to expect on margins for the next 12 months through guidance that was "good" and added clarity, Klein noted.

So why don't investors seem very interested in the stock?

It's a dynamic that's "unexplainable," Klein wrote on Friday, noting he's gotten "zero good answers" when he's posed the question lately in nearly all of his investor conversations.

But whether there are no answers or just no good answers may be more of the issue. Klein does have a few theories around why investors may be dodging Microsoft's stock lately. For one, they may be worried about how Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing growth will fare once OpenAI "jumps ship and takes their act on road solo." OpenAI reportedly has a $300 billion cloud deal with Oracle Corp. (ORCL) over the coming years that "implies they want off ... Azure sooner vs. later," according to Klein.

Read: Why the $300 billion Oracle-OpenAI deal could be fueling an AI bubble

There's also the fact that Microsoft is a software company, and Klein has seen some investors rotate out of the software sector in favor of semiconductors.

Evercore ISI analyst Kirk Materne explored the phenomenon as well in a note to clients last week. At the time of his Sept. 9 report, he said he was starting to get more investor questions about Microsoft's lagging postearnings stock performance relative to the S&P 500 SPX.

"On a fundamental basis, we don't see much to justify the recent underperformance," he wrote. "That said, the new 'marketecture' around the 'Mag 7' means that [Microsoft] will, at times, lose some momentum if another 'Mag 7' member is seeing a material shift in sentiment" - which had been happening as Apple Inc.'s stock (AAPL) began to see new shine in the eyes of investors.

Materne noted there was a high bar for Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing unit after the company stunned Wall Street with 39% growth last quarter, "but the trends underpinning that growth - namely ChatGPT inferencing and solid enterprise demand - seem stable."

Don't miss: Why Microsoft's 'must-own' stock looks even more like a no-brainer after earnings

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: 89bio Cut to Market Perform From Outperform by Raymond James
Nike Gets Upgrade As World Cup Boost Looms
data.description:
Nike just scored a big upgrade. RBC Capital lifted the stock to Outperform from Sector Perform and bumped its price target by 18% to $90, betting the 2026 FIFA World Cup and a stronger product lineup could kickstart a sharper revenue recovery.

Analyst Piral Dadhania says Nike is set to lean on its hefty marketing budgetnearly $5 billion for FY26, about twice adidas (ADDYY)to flood the World Cup stage with visibility. With sponsorship of 6 of the top 10 FIFA-ranked teams, including France, England and Brazil, the payoff could reach $1.3 billion, spread mostly across FY26. Adidas is expected to sponsor 4 top teams by comparison.

RBC expects Nike to use the tournament as a springboard for fresh launches, from new kits in March 2026 to revamped apparel later that year. That could add momentum to CEO Elliott Hill's turnaround strategy, which is already showing signs of traction.

Nike reports fiscal Q1 on Sept. 30, with Wall Street bracing for EPS of $0.27 on revenue of $10.96 billion, down 15% year over year but nearly double last quarter's profit.

data.title: xAI raises $10 billion at $200 billion valuation, CNBC reports
data.description:
Elon Musk's AI firm xAI has raised $10 billion at a $200 billion valuation, CNBC reported on Friday, citing sources.

xAI did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

Morgan Stanley had in late June said that xAI had completed a $5 billion debt raise alongside a separate $5 billion strategic equity investment, as the startup looked to expand its AI infrastructure through data centers amid intense competition.

data.title: UPS Walks Away From Estafeta Deal After Hurdles
data.description:
United Parcel Service has walked away from its plan to buy Mexican delivery firm Estafeta, saying key conditions of the deal couldn't be met. The decision was made public Thursday in an 8-K filing.

The move is a setback for UPS, which had hoped the acquisition would give it a stronger foothold in Latin America at a time when e-commerce growth is fueling demand for faster, more reliable logistics. Instead, the company will need to look for other ways to deepen its presence in Mexico and the broader region.

For investors, the breakdown underscores how even big players like UPS can hit hurdles in cross-border deals. With growth slowing in its core U.S. market, international expansion is still a key lever, but this missed opportunity may push timelines back.

data.title: Porsche Cuts Guidance Again After EV Market Woes Prompt Lineup Review — Update
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo and Mauro Orru

Porsche AG cut its guidance for the year again as it continues to reel from a slow electric-vehicle market, fierce competition from Chinese rivals and President Trump's tariffs.

The German premium-car maker said it had been forced to conduct a significant review of its product portfolio, delaying the launch of some new fully electric vehicle models and adding models with combustion and hybrid drivetrains to supplement its lineup.

A new SUV Series won't initially be available as all-electric vehicles as originally planned. Instead, those vehicles will be offered exclusively as combustion engines and plug-in hybrids. Current models such as the Panamera and the Cayenne will also be available with combustion engines and as plug-in hybrids well into the 2030s.

The group expects to spend around 3.1 billion euros ($2.12 billion) on its realignment. Of this, up to 1.8 billion euros will go toward the rescheduling of a new platform for electric vehicles. As a result, Porsche now expects a return on sales of up to 2% this year, down from a previous forecast range of 5% to 7%.

It also lowered its automotive earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization margin guidance to between 10.5% and 12.5%, from a previous range of 14.5% to 16.5%. It continues to expect sales between 37 billion euros and 38 billion euros.

Porsche has been grappling with a slow EV market. Last year, it scaled back its EV ambitions and vowed to expand its portfolio in the coming years to include models with combustion engines and plug-in hybrid powertrains.

Earlier this year, Porsche unveiled plans to shed around 3,900 jobs by 2029 in a bid to become more efficient and inject new life into the brand.

Aside from EV market woes and declining demand in China, Porsche is also reckoning with 27.5% import tariffs in the U.S. President Trump plans to keep those duties on EU auto imports until the bloc introduces legislation to reduce levies on a variety of U.S. goods.

The company slashed guidance in July and April as it warned investors tariffs would have a negative effect. Its decision to cut its outlook for the second time in less than two months on Friday had ripple effects on Volkswagen and Porsche SE, the entity controlled by the Porsche-Piech family that holds big stakes in both companies.

Volkswagen, which owns more than 75% of Porsche AG, said its operating profit this year would take a hit of around 5.1 billion euros because of Porsche's forecast adjustments and portfolio review. Volkswagen now expects an operating return on sales to between 2% and 3% this year, compared with 4% to 5% previously.

Meanwhile, Porsche SE--which has a direct equity investment of around 12.5% in Porsche AG--is expecting an adjusted after tax profit of 900 million euros to 2.9 billion euros this year, below previous guidance of 1.6 billion euros to 3.6 billion euros.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com and Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

Wheat Futures Ease on Global Supply Boost
data.description:
Wheat futures slipped toward $5.20 per bushel, extending a two-day retreat from a 1-1/5-month high of $5.35, as traders weighed domestic and global supply prospects.

Forecasted showers across the Midwest and north Delta are expected to benefit US soft wheat crops, while Western Australia raised its output outlook by 300,000 metric tons.

Adding to the bearish tone, the International Grains Council boosted its 2025/26 global production forecast by 8 million metric tons to 819 million.

These supply-side increases have pressured sentiment, though US wheat has pared some of its earlier losses this week.

Overall, futures remain steady but face ongoing headwinds from abundant global production and improving crop conditions.

data.title: Venezuela's pattern of arrests of opposition members' relatives meant to create fear, UN says
data.description:
Venezuela arrests opposition figures' family members in a "sustained and systematic pattern" to generate fear and social control, a United Nations fact-finding mission said in an upcoming report.

The report, seen by Reuters on Friday, is set to be presented at a session of the U.N. Human Rights Council next week. It is based on research conducted from September 2024 through August this year.

The South American country's opposition and international human rights bodies have denounced an escalating crackdown on opposition figures there since a July 2024 presidential election.

The opposition published ballot box tallies showing a resounding victory for its candidate, but the government of President Nicolas Maduro and the supreme court said Maduro won a third term.

Neither the communications ministry nor the attorney general's office responded immediately to requests for comment.

Maduro's government has previously said that the U.N.'s fact-finding mission is a mechanism of aggression, and has insisted that Venezuela is a democracy governed by laws.

Arrests of relatives of "people from the opposition or perceived as such ... are not limited to individual actions, but respond to a policy of repression oriented towards creating fear and social control," the report said.

Family members are detained as reprisals against opposition politicians or as a way to exert pressure, the report said, and the arrests have serious impacts on family units and reinforce a climate of fear.

Among those named in the report is Rafael Tudares, son-in-law of the opposition's former presidential candidate Edmundo Gonzalez, who was detained in January while taking his young children to school. His family says they have received no information about him since.

Relatives in the cases probed by the U.N. were denied access to a lawyer of their choice and instead were given public defenders, the report said.

data.title: U.S. Oil Rig Count Rises for Fourth Week — Market Talk
data.description: The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1332 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1310 ET - Expedia executives see artificial intelligence as a means to reinvent the company again, following previous transformations with a shift to mobile and the rise of the internet, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending an event with CEO Ariane Gorin. The online travel agency benefits from a swath of data and has started to incorporate AI across its platform to summarize guest reviews and answer questions about properties, the analysts say. Those on-platform tools have already led to a clear improvement in conversion, though it's still too early to assess the impact from the company's AI agents and other external AI tools, the analysts say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1302 ET - The housing market is stuck in a vicious cycle in which low housing supply leads to high prices, which discourages home sales, which then leads builders to pull back on increasing supply, Lennar's Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "New construction has slowed as builders have pulled back on production due to slow sales and affordability concerns, therefore exacerbating the chronic supply shortage," Miller says, adding that demand is still high with people wanting and needing homes. "This has been a difficult cycle, as low supply fuels high prices and high prices lock out many of our buyers." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Bill Holdings is due for a bull run as the company tries to outperform low Wall Street growth forecasts, Truist analysts say in a research note as they upgrade shares to buy from hold. The business-assistance software company is set up to exceed analyst expectations in core revenue growth with both easier comps ahead and better-than-expected customer growth in its Embedded Solutions segment, the analysts say. Bill stock has been a laggard in the fintech sector for the past couple of years due to both a lack of investor interest in small- to mid-cap fintech companies and a slowdown in core revenue growth, but that has reset the stock low enough for investors to become bullish again, the analysts say. They also see sizable long positions held by activist shareholders as a plus. Shares rise 0.6% to $52.90. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1153 ET - BlackBerry should be able to achieve or beat its guidance in its fiscal 2Q, CIBC's Todd Coupland says in a report. He says BlackBerry's average surprise compared with FactSet is 7% for revenue and almost triple for adjusted EBITDA since John Giamatteo took the helm as CEO at the end of 2023. "We expect this encouraging trend to continue," he says, noting thatshow progress in secure communications, its secure messaging and cybersecurity platform for governments and enterprises, as well as QNX, which is an embedded operating system that is used in automotive, medical and industrial applications. "We see these trends accelerating throughout F2026," Coupland adds. BlackBerry reports its 2Q on Sept. 25. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Greater adoption of artificial intelligence tools is the leading trend in travel, with the number of consumers who used an AI-based app to help with trip planning more than doubling year-over-year to about 30% in 2025, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending a panel with industry executives. While current experimentation of agentic AI in the travel industry is widespread, most companies are using the same undifferentiated AI chatbots, which have yet to materialize into any economic benefit, the analysts say. Companies should be able to monetize AI in the future with vertical use cases, like automating procurement or reimagining call centers, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1116 ET - Cracker Barrel abandoned its logo change and restaurant redesign, but other elements of the company's transformation plan will be leaned into more heavily, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says in a research note. Those elements have been working and can drive both increased traffic and customer frequency as well as stronger profitability, the analyst says. Management has already teased that it will double down on its menu innovation efforts, which have proven successful for the brand, Brooks says. The company will also continue to work on back-of-house efficiency efforts and drive further impact from the brand's 9 million-member loyalty program, whose members drive more than a third of Cracker Barrel's revenue, the analyst says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1105 ET - Cracker Barrel Old Country Store is going to have to use additional marketing and discounting efforts to drive disgruntled customers back to the brand or find new ones, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says in a research note. Since the company tried to change its logo and update its branding in mid-August, customer pushback has caused traffic trends to fall from being down 1% in the first half of the year to being down 8% currently, the analyst says. The challenge now will be defining how long this traffic loss lasts after the company's hard pivot back to embracing its nostalgic branding and restaurant design, the analyst says. Shares fall 2% to $44.86. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1048 ET - Kohl's shares are poised to continue recovering from a multi-decade low in April as it faces easing year-over-year comparisons in the back half of the fiscal year, Citi analysts say in a research note. Web traffic data shows some acceleration in 3Q, leading the analysts to raise their comparable sales forecast. Analysts say they also have greater confidence in the company's ability to generate free cash flow. Kohl's doesn't have any major debt maturities until 2030. While 2H will present some macro challenges and longer-term secular issues remain, near-term upside and high short interest in the stock should move shares higher in the near term, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1029 ET - Base metal prices are mixed in afternoon trade, with a firmer U.S. dollar weighing on the complex. Copper futures on the London Metal Exchange are up 0.3% to $9,975 a metric ton, while aluminum falls 1.3% to $2,671.50 a ton, with market watchers saying any benefit from U.S. rate cuts might be delayed until real economic activity picks up. "The picture on the aluminum market remains unclear," says Commerzbank's Thu Lan Nguyen. "After the metal received a boost last week from a sharp rise in cancelled warrants on the LME, inventories recently rose again more strongly, dampening hopes of a tightening market." In copper news, Nguyen points out that if the International Copper Study Group reveals next week that the copper market was notably oversupplied in the first seven months of 2025, it could delay a near-term recovery in prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0948 ET - Cruise lines appear to be in for a mixed second half of the year, Truist analyst C. Patrick Scholes says. Scholes expects cruise companies not to disappoint with 3Q earnings and to maintain full-year guides, but he also sees an uptick in promotions and price cuts that could make it difficult for companies to beat expectations. At the start of the year, Scholes had been expecting outperformance. He now thinks growth rates are normalizing, based on booking and pricing data. "We would describe our latest observations as having 'mixed implications' for the industry/stocks," he says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

-0

0907 ET - Aumovio's obligation to compensate BMW on its brake recall last year might be a stain on the company for some investors, UBS analysts write in a note. The Continental AG spinoff went public with a $4.1 billion valuation Thursday. Although UBS analysts cut the company's valuation by 700 million euros due to the fact that parent Continental supplied the faulty parts, the analysts consider compensation a manageable risk. The two companies could find a commercial agreement that softens the hit on Aumovio, they say. Aumovio shares are up 5.3% at 40.68 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

data.title: Soybean futures fall after Trump-Xi call; corn, wheat sag
data.description:
By Julie Ingwersen

U.S. soybean futures fell to their lowest in a week on Friday, erasing early gains after a phone call between U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping failed to yield news on soybean exports, analysts said.

Corn and wheat futures followed soybeans lower. Additional pressure stemmed from the ongoing Midwest harvest, even as market players debate the size of this year's corn and soy production.

As of 12:12 p.m. CDT (1712 GMT), Chicago Board of Trade November soybeans (SX25) were down 11-1/4 cents at $10.26-1/4 per bushel after hitting $10.25, near the contract's 50-day moving average and its lowest level since September 11.

CBOT December corn was down 1 cent at $4.22-3/4 a bushel and December wheat (WZ25) was down 3-3/4 cents at $5.20-1/2 a bushel.

Soybeans rose early on hopes that the Trump-Xi call could help end a freeze in soybean shipments between the two countries. China, by far the world's largest soy importer, has yet to book any of the current U.S. soybean crop and has instead turned to South American supplies.

The two leaders made progress during the call on a TikTok agreement and pledged to meet face to face in just over a month in South Korea , but there was little mention of agriculture. CBOT soybean futures turned lower after the call ended.

"The market had been hoping for a lot more positive outcome from these talks," said Ted Seifried, chief strategist for Zaner Ag Hedge. "It doesn't sound like (U.S.) soybean sales are going to be happening any time soon, and that is putting a fair amount of pressure on markets."

The harvest is expanding in the U.S. corn belt. Unusually warm temperatures this week are pushing crops toward maturity, the U.S. Department of Agriculture said in a daily weather note, with highs near 90 degrees Fahrenheit (32 Celsius) as far north as the Ohio River Valley.

Traders are monitoring early yield reports to gauge the impact of crop diseases and late-summer dryness that could prompt the USDA to lower its yield estimates next month.

Meanwhile, a rebound in the dollar since a U.S. Federal Reserve interest rate cut on Wednesday also weighed on dollar-priced commodities.

Wheat futures were anchored by improving wheat harvest prospects in Australia and globally .

data.title: Judge Scraps Trump's $15 Billion New York Times Defamation Lawsuit — WSJ
data.description:
By Louise Radnofsky

A federal judge on Friday struck President Trump's $15 billion defamation lawsuit against the New York Times, characterizing the president's legal complaint as a vitriolic screed that violated basic court requirements for suing someone.

U.S. Judge Steven Merryday's order striking the complaint came just four days after it was filed by the president in the Middle District of Florida. He gave the president's lawyers 28 days to file a new version.

"As every lawyer knows (or is presumed to know), a complaint is not a public forum for vituperation and invective — not a protected platform to rage against an adversary," Merryday wrote. "A complaint is not a megaphone for public relations or a podium for a passionate oration at a political rally or the functional equivalent of the Hyde Park Speakers' Corner."

Trump's lawsuit had named as defendants the Times and four of its reporters, alongside book publisher Penguin Random House. It accused the paper of interference in the 2024 presidential election and of "spreading false and defamatory content" about Trump.

The complaint cited several Times articles and singled out a book written by reporters of the newspaper and published by Penguin Random House titled "Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success."

The judge, who was appointed to the bench by George H.W. Bush, called the complaint "decidedly improper and impermissible."

Merryday noted that while the lawsuit alleged two straightforward counts of defamation, it "consumes eighty-five pages. Count I appears on page eighty, and Count II appears on page eighty-three," he said. The complaint, he added, contains "many, often repetitive, and laudatory (toward President Trump) but superfluous allegations."

A lawyer for Trump who signed the complaint didn't immediately respond to a request for comment, nor did the New York Times or Penguin Random House. The Times had previously said the suit "lacks any legitimate legal claims and instead is an attempt to stifle and discourage independent reporting."

Trump has filed a separate lawsuit against the publisher of The Wall Street Journal in the Southern District of Florida, alleging the newspaper defamed him in an article about a birthday letter sent to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein. A Dow Jones spokeswoman has said of that case, "We have full confidence in the rigor and accuracy of our reporting, and will vigorously defend against any lawsuit."

Merryday said that the president's legal team can file an amended complaint within 28 days, but cautioned: "This action will begin, will continue, and will end in accord with the rules of procedure and in a professional and dignified manner."

The amended complaint, he concluded, must not exceed 40 pages, "excluding only the caption, the signature, and any attachment."

Write to Louise Radnofsky at louise.radnofsky@wsj.com

data.title: Trump has a chance to do what he claims to be so good at: make a deal with China on energy
data.description:
By Paul Brandus

How a natural-gas deal could boost energy stocks and U.S.-China relations

Chinese President Xi Jinping and U.S. President Donald Trump have something the other wants: natural gas. China needs it; the U.S. has it.

The recent meeting in China among leaders of an "Axis of Opposition" - China, Russia, India and other countries that resent American power and the U.S.-led world order - was heavy on geopolitical symbolism. But for investors, the real story, underneath the photo ops and champagne toasts, concerns energy.

With Russian President Vladimir Putin apparently intending to continue the war he started in Ukraine, he's scrambling to finance it. Oil and gas are what he has to sell, and fortunately for him, India and China - the world's two most populous countries - are energy-hungry buyers. This year, India and China have each been buying about 2 million barrels of crude a day from Russia, according to data from the International Energy Agency. That's 4 million barrels of discounted oil that China and India won't need to buy on the open market.

China and India know they have Putin in a corner, and they're squeezing Moscow. Indian refiners reportedly want even steeper discounts to compensate for the risks they take in dealing with Russia, such as compliance, reputational and banking.

President Donald Trump, angling perhaps for sales of crude from the U.S. - the world's largest producer of oil - doesn't want any Russian oil sales to India. An original 25% tariff on Indian exports to the U.S. was doubled by Trump after New Delhi refused to halt its oil imports.

The problem with Trump's demand is that it's bumping up against a prior American policy, coordinated with other G-7 nations, to allow Russian oil exports to India, China and other buyers such as Turkey, through a price-cap scheme. The idea was to squeeze Putin's war machine by forcing him to sell at lower prices.

Why not really put the screws to the Kremlin by hitting Russia with a total embargo? Because allowing Putin to sell oil maintains an overall equilibrium in global markets while simultaneously preventing energy-price shocks. Such an embargo could drive prices up globally - and raise gasoline prices in the U.S. That's probably the last thing wanted by Trump, who is anxious to keep inflation in check. So far, gasoline prices have risen slightly since he took office, inching up about 6 cents to a nationwide average of $3.26 a gallon, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (a federal agency).

But what's interesting here -and perhaps could actually support Trump's idea of a deeper oil embargo against Russia - is that oil prices have fallen sharply since Putin went into Ukraine more than 31/2 years ago. Brent crude (BRN00), for example, traded at about $98 a barrel in February 2022; it's now in the upper $60s. West Texas Intermediate (WBS00)? Similar story: $91 to about $63.

A tighter embargo on Putin now would inflict more pain on the struggling Russian economy - perhaps weakening his position vis-a-vis Ukraine - and would also raise global prices. But since Brent and WTI are priced around a third lower than when the Ukraine war started, Western consumers could endure a modest price hike. Just an idea. This helps explain why Putin is eager, perhaps desperately so, to sell even more oil to China.

Read: Russia and China's gas alliance is a bluff. Will Washington call it?

Energy investors should also keep their eye on Russia's ongoing scramble to find new customers for its natural gas. The European Union says it obtained about 45% of its gas from Putin before the war started; late last year that was down to 19%, and the E.U.'s goal is to phase out Russian natural gas completely by 2027. What was once Putin's biggest customer will soon be gone.

What will Putin do? Running out of customers, Putin is pivoting to China. He's desperate for the so-called Power of Siberia 2 pipeline to be built. It would carry natural gas some 1,600 miles through Siberia, through Mongolia, and into northern China. But like India, which is using Moscow's economic weakness as leverage, China's President Xi Jinping also knows how to squeeze the Russians. He has dragged his heels on the project for years.

The pipeline "could reshape global energy" and supply more than a fifth of China's projected gas needs in 2030, according to a recent analysis by the Washington-based Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS). That's if it gets built. Chinese and Russian officials have signed what's described as a "legally binding memorandum" - though the CSIS analysis said that "Reportedly, no agreement has been reached on price, timeline, or builder." It could be years before Russian gas flows to China and money comes to Moscow. That's too bad for Putin. He's playing for time; but that doesn't seem to be Xi's problem.

Once again, there's an American angle. Trump has made no secret of his desire to boost exports of liquefied natural gas (LNG) to the world. Qatar and Australia are now the biggest suppliers of LNG to China, and Trump would love a piece of that action. What might this mean for broader U.S.-Chinese issues, such as the ongoing tariff spat between Washington and Beijing? The world's two economic giants have extended their pause on higher tariffs through early November. Both Trump and Xi have something the other wants. China needs gas, the U.S. has it. Here's an opportunity for Trump to do what he claims to be good at: making a deal.

More: Foreign investors are still buying U.S. assets in 2025 - but there is one catch

Also read: China and Russia vie to dominate in energy. Trump Is handing them the win.

-Paul Brandus

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Elon Musk's xAI Raises $10 Billion At $200 Billion Valuation - CNBC Citing Sources

data.title: Russian jets violated safety zone of drilling platform in Baltic sea, Poland says
data.description:
Two Russian fighter jets violated the safety zone of the Petrobaltic drilling platform in the Baltic sea, Polish Border Guards said on Friday, adding that the Armed Forces and other services had been notified.

Estonia earlier said three Russian military jets had entered its airspace on Friday for 12 minutes in an "unprecedentedly brazen" incursion, amid rising tensions on NATO's eastern flank.

The Polish Border Guard wrote on X: "Two Russian fighter jets performed a low pass over the Petrobaltic platform in the Baltic Sea. The platform's safety zone was violated."

Last week Poland shot down suspected Russian drones in its airspace with the backing of aircraft from its NATO allies, the first time a member of the Western military alliance is known to have fired shots during Russia's war in Ukraine.

data.title: Ipiranga sales jump after raids targeting organized crime in Brazil fuel sector
data.description:
Brazilian fuel distributor Ipiranga has seen its sales volumes rise after police and tax authorities carried out raids to dismantle an organized crime ring in the sector, the company's chief executive said on Friday.

Multinational energy companies have struggled for years to root out organized crime from their distribution networks in Brazil, and experts had projected the crackdown to result in gains for major fuel distributors.

Ipiranga's sales in Sao Paulo state, Brazil's richest and most populous, are up after the raids, CEO Leonardo Linden said, though he said it was too soon to measure the full impact of the raids on the company's margins.

"I expect gains in (market share) in August and especially in September," he said.

Last month, authorities in Latin America's largest economy launched a series of raids aimed at dismantling multibillion-dollar money laundering and fraud schemes linked to organized crime in the fuel sector.

Firms targeted in the raids had a 7% market share of the country's fuel distribution market, while holding a 33% share of Sao Paulo's ethanol market, according to Ipiranga, which is controlled by conglomerate Ultra .

Brazil's Instituto Combustivel Legal - an industry group created to combat fuel fraud - had said it expected sales volume for major distributors, including Ipiranga, to rise as a result of the raids.

"I've never seen such a significant police operation. ... But it can't stop here," Linden said. "We need to build a more balanced environment that better encourages companies that do things right in the sector."

data.title: Coca-Cola Bottler Warns 600 South Africa Jobs At Risk
data.description:
Coca-Cola is facing a storm in South Africa, where its local bottler has warned more than 600 workers their jobs could be on the line. Coca-Cola Beverages South Africa has begun a consultation process with unions after sending out retrenchment notices, Business Day reported. No final call has been made yet.

Union spokesperson Dominique Swartz said the company pointed to financial pressures as the main reason. As part of the shake-up, CCBSA is also preparing to shut down its Bloemfontein and East London plants, moves that underline how squeezed operations have become.

For Coca-Cola, the cuts show the strain of running in a tough economic environment where costs are rising and demand is uneven. But trimming jobs and closing plants risks fueling anger from unions and hurting its image in a key African market.

Investors will be watching how the talks play outand whether Coca-Cola can cut costs without sparking deeper pushback from workers and communities.

data.title: Lennar Misses Q3 Sales and Profit Estimates
data.description:
Lennar stock is under duress after the homebuilder missed Wall Street's marks on both profit and sales. The latest results show how rising rates and affordability issues continue to pinch the housing market.

For the fiscal third quarter, Lennar reported non-GAAP EPS of $2.00, about $0.09 shy of estimates. Revenue came in at $8.81 billion, down more than 6% from a year ago. Home sales dropped 9% to $8.2 billion, while the backlog slipped to 16,953 homes worth $6.6 billion. The Multifamily business turned in a $16 million loss versus $79 million in profit a year earlier, though Financial Services provided a lift with $177 million in earnings, up from $144 million.

The company is guiding for 20,00021,000 new orders in Q4, with average sales prices of $380K to $390K. But the stock has already been under pressure, down 2% this year while the S&P 500 has gained 12%.

For investors, the numbers paint a picture of a builder caught between strong long-term demand and the near-term drag of higher borrowing costs.
